perturbation.original_text: Recipient hereby acknowledges and agrees that no remedy at law will afford Discloser adequate protection against, or appropriate compensation for, breach of Recipient’s obligations under this Agreement. Accordingly, Recipient agrees that Discloser shall be entitled to specific performance of Recipient’s obligations, as well as such further equitable relief as may be granted by a court of competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient hereby acknowledges and agrees that Discloser's remedies are limited to monetary damages. Accordingly, Recipient's liability is capped at $1000.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the Discloser's right to seek specific performance or other equitable relief, limiting remedies to monetary damages and capping liability. This is a significant departure from standard legal practice and may violate principles of adequate remedy.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification directly contradicts California Civil Code § 3368, which states that specific performance may be compelled unless it is denied by a special provision of law. By limiting the Discloser's remedy to a capped monetary amount, the agreement effectively eliminates the availability of specific performance as a remedy, which may be required to adequately protect the Discloser's interests in preserving confidential information, and in contradiction with Cal. Civ. Code § 3368.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3368 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. General Principles [3366 - 3369] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 3368. Preventive relief is given by prohibiting a party from doing that which ought not to be done. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original text allows for specific performance, while the changed text limits remedies to monetary damages capped at $1000. The Law Explanation states this may contradict California Civil Code § 3368, which allows specific performance unless denied by law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows CIV 3368 states "Preventive relief is given by prohibiting a party from doing that which ought not to be done." By limiting the Discloser's remedy to monetary damages, the agreement eliminates the possibility of specific performance which is a form of preventive relief.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of California.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by the laws of the State of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Nevada could affect the enforceability of certain clauses, especially regarding non-compete or confidentiality provisions. Some California laws are more protective of employee rights than Nevada laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 16600 generally prohibits non-compete agreements. While this is a confidentiality agreement, changing the governing law to Nevada could open the door to interpreting clauses in a way that might be considered a prohibited restraint of trade under California law, especially if the agreement is later interpreted to restrict future employment. By changing the governing law, this circumvents the protections afforded by Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600 if the Recipient is in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the governing law from California to Nevada, which creates a legal contradiction. According to the Law Explanation, this change could circumvent the protections afforded by Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code § 16600 if the Recipient is in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Code, BPC 16600(a), states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.", suggesting California has a strong stance against non-compete agreements. By changing the governing law, the modified text potentially weakens the protection offered by California law, creating a contradiction. The omission of California law in favor of Nevada law in this context leads to a legal loophole that could undermine employee rights in California.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall give Discloser written notice prior to such disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall make reasonable efforts to give Discloser notice prior to such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written notice' to 'reasonable efforts to give notice' weakens the requirement to notify the Discloser before disclosing Confidential Information in response to a judicial order. This may violate the Discloser's rights if 'reasonable efforts' is not interpreted as providing timely and adequate notice to allow the Discloser to take protective action.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1985.3 outlines specific procedures for subpoenaing a consumer's personal records, including notice requirements to the consumer. While this section deals with subpoenas, the principle of adequate notice to allow a party to protect their interests is fundamental. By weakening the notice requirement to 'reasonable efforts,' the agreement potentially allows disclosure without giving the Discloser sufficient time to challenge the disclosure order or seek a protective order, violating the spirit of procedural due process implicit in CCP § 1985.3.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1985.3 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 3. OF THE PRODUCTION OF EVIDENCE [1985 - 2015.6] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 2. Means of Production [1985 - 1997] ( Chapter 2 enacted 1872. ) 1985.3. (a) For purposes of this section, the following definitions apply: (1) “Personal records” means the original, any copy of books, documents, other writings, or electronically stored information pertaining to a consumer and which are maintained by any “witness” which is a physician, dentist, ophthalmologist, optometrist, chiropractor, physical therapist, acupuncturist, podiatrist, veterinarian, veterinary hospital, veterinary clinic, pharmacist, pharmacy, hospital, medical center, clinic, radiology or MRI center, clinical or diagnostic laboratory, state or national bank, state or federal association (as defined in Section 5102 of the Financial Code), state or federal credit union, trust company, anyone authorized by this state to make or arrange loans that are secured by real property, security brokerage firm, insurance company, title insurance company, underwritten title company, escrow agent licensed pursuant to Division 6 (commencing with Section 17000) of the Financial Code or exempt fro
perturbation.justification: The change from 'written notice' to 'reasonable efforts to give notice' weakens the original requirement and creates a contradiction. While the law snippet (CCP 1985.3) focuses on subpoenas and personal records, the Law Explanation correctly points out that the 'principle of adequate notice to allow a party to protect their interests is fundamental.' The modification allows for potential disclosure without sufficient time for the Discloser to challenge the order, undermining the Discloser's ability to take protective action. The original text ensured a higher standard of notification, while the modified text introduces ambiguity and lowers the standard of care, thus contradicting the spirit of procedural due process implicit in CCP § 1985.3, by potentially not providing adequate time to respond to disclosures.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the request of Discloser, Recipient shall, at Recipient’s option, either (a) return such materials to Discloser, or (b) certify the destruction thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the request of Discloser, Recipient may, at Recipient’s option, return such materials to Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the obligation to either return or certify the destruction of confidential information, making it optional for the recipient to do so. This weakens the Discloser's control over its confidential information and creates a risk of continued possession and potential misuse of the information by the Recipient after the agreement terminates.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: Although not directly stating the action to be taken, this directly violates the Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which protects the owners of Trade Secret information. By weakening the clause regarding the protection and return of confidential information, the risk of continued possession and use of trade secrets increases, undermining the protections afforded by CUTSA. While CUTSA doesn't mandate return/destruction, weakening the agreed-upon methods increases the likelihood of misappropriation claims.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the Recipient to either return or destroy confidential information upon Discloser's request. The changed text weakens this obligation by making it optional for the Recipient to return the materials, removing the destruction option entirely. The Law Explanation suggests this weakening increases the risk of misappropriation claims under the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1 cites Cal. Civ. Code § 3426, the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. While the snippet only identifies the act's citation, the explanation argues the change undermines the protections afforded by CUTSA. The omission of the mandatory 'return or certify destruction' creates a higher risk for trade secret misuse compared to the original agreement, establishing a contradiction as defined in Omission Legal Contradiction.
